item.title: RSUS: A Novel Upsampling Layer for Semantic Segmentation Network of Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Yaning Liu
Ronghao Yang
Shaoda Li
Junxiang Tan
Guyue Hu
Chenghao Liu
Yunfan Luo
Zhiqiu Liang
Tian Dong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Semantic segmentation of remote sensing images often struggles with boundary blurring, structural discontinuity, and category confusion due to limitations in conventional interpolation and dynamic upsampling methods. This paper proposes RSUS, a new upsampling layer designed to preserve semantic consistency and spatial structure. RSUS consists of three components: (1) Global Context Vector Aggregation (GCVA) for content-aware prediction kernels that introduce global priors into local feature reconstruction, (2) Cross-scale Anisotropic Implicit Positional Encoding (CAIPE) for direction-sensitive spatial deformation and structural alignment, and (3) Adaptive High-frequency Structure Gating (AHSG) to enhance boundary-related frequency responses. Additionally, Persistent Homology (PH) is used as a topological analysis tool to assess connectivity and structure preservation beyond pixel-level metrics. Extensive experiments on five datasets (ISPRS Vaihingen, ISPRS Potsdam, LoveDA, UAVid, and Xining) demonstrate that RSUS improves performance across mainstream segmentation networks, outperforming advanced upsampling methods. Analyses show RSUS alleviates structural misalignment, improves category consistency, and recovers fine-grained boundaries in remote sensing segmentation. The source code is available at：https://github.com/Ronin-711/RSUS. Published: 2026-07-17T19:44:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yaning Liu; Ronghao Yang; Shaoda Li; Junxiang Tan; Guyue Hu; Chenghao Liu; Yunfan Luo; Zhiqiu Liang; Tian Dong

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3714102

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Semantic segmentation of remote sensing images often struggles with boundary blurring, structural discontinuity, and category confusion due to limitations in conventional interpolation and dynamic upsampling methods. This paper proposes RSUS, a new upsampling layer designed to preserve semantic consistency and spatial structure. RSUS consists of three components: (1) Global Context Vector Aggregation (GCVA) for content-aware prediction kernels that introduce global priors into local feature reconstruction, (2) Cross-scale Anisotropic Implicit Positional Encoding (CAIPE) for direction-sensitive spatial deformation and structural alignment, and (3) Adaptive High-frequency Structure Gating (AHSG) to enhance boundary-related frequency responses. Additionally, Persistent Homology (PH) is used as a topological analysis tool to assess connectivity and structure preservation beyond pixel-level metrics. Extensive experiments on five datasets (ISPRS Vaihingen, ISPRS Potsdam, LoveDA, UAVid, and Xining) demonstrate that RSUS improves performance across mainstream segmentation networks, outperforming advanced upsampling methods. Analyses show RSUS alleviates structural misalignment, improves category consistency, and recovers fine-grained boundaries in remote sensing segmentation. The source code is available at：https://github.com/Ronin-711/RSUS.

item.title: ARAS-Net: A Meta-Learning-Based Few-Shot Small Target Detection Network for Aerial Infrared Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Lianmeng Jiao
Haifeng Wei
Quan Pan
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Recent advances in infrared small target detection and few-shot learning have yielded promising outcomes; however, the existing approaches address these challenges separately, resulting in limited generalization and robustness in complex aerial remote sensing environments. To overcome this limitation, we propose ARAS-Net, a meta-learning-based few-shot small target detection framework tailored for aerial infrared remote sensing images. The proposed method incorporates an attention-guided context mechanism that enhances the distinction between targets and background in cluttered scenes, a mutual aggregation strategy that promotes rapid adaptation and generalization under limited sample conditions, and a multi-scale feature enhancement design that effectively captures and represents small targets. Comprehensive experiments on benchmark aerial infrared datasets confirm that our approach consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods in both detection accuracy and robustness under few-shot settings. The source code is available at https://github.com/jlm-138/ARAS-Net. Published: 2026-07-16T21:49:55+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lianmeng Jiao; Haifeng Wei; Quan Pan

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3713883

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Recent advances in infrared small target detection and few-shot learning have yielded promising outcomes; however, the existing approaches address these challenges separately, resulting in limited generalization and robustness in complex aerial remote sensing environments. To overcome this limitation, we propose ARAS-Net, a meta-learning-based few-shot small target detection framework tailored for aerial infrared remote sensing images. The proposed method incorporates an attention-guided context mechanism that enhances the distinction between targets and background in cluttered scenes, a mutual aggregation strategy that promotes rapid adaptation and generalization under limited sample conditions, and a multi-scale feature enhancement design that effectively captures and represents small targets. Comprehensive experiments on benchmark aerial infrared datasets confirm that our approach consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods in both detection accuracy and robustness under few-shot settings. The source code is available at https://github.com/jlm-138/ARAS-Net.

item.title: Two-Stage Unpaired Image Dehazing via Real-Driven Haze Transfer and Physics-Aware Constraint
item.dc:creator: Shuanhu Di
Jiali Rong
Miao Liao
Xin Xu
Xinglong Zhang
Qiang Fang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Image dehazing is critical for enhancing the visual quality of images acquired in hazy environments. However, due to the scarcity of real-world hazy image datasets, most existing dehazing methods rely on paired synthetic datasets, leading to limited generalization performance when applied to real images. In this paper, we propose a novel two-stage unpaired dehazing framework, which unifies real-driven haze transfer and physics-aware constraint to address the domain gap between synthetic and real hazy images. Specifically, in the first stage, a haze-transfer network is designed to generate depth-aware pseudo hazy images by fusing haze characteristics learned from real hazy images with depth cues extracted from unpaired clear images, narrowing the distribution discrepancy between real and synthetic haze. In the second stage, a physics-inspired three-branch network is developed, including a transmission map network, an atmospheric light network, and a scene radiance network. A joint optimization strategy, leveraging pseudo hazy-clear pairs and reconstruction constraints based on the atmospheric model, is proposed to enhance both dehazing performance and model interpretability. Comprehensive experiments conducted on SOTS-outdoor, HSTS, Fattal, and BeDDE datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms many existing state-of-the-art unpaired dehazing methods. The code is available at:https://github.com/Marinerjl/TU-Dehaze. Published: 2026-07-17T19:46:42+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shuanhu Di; Jiali Rong; Miao Liao; Xin Xu; Xinglong Zhang; Qiang Fang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3714452

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Image dehazing is critical for enhancing the visual quality of images acquired in hazy environments. However, due to the scarcity of real-world hazy image datasets, most existing dehazing methods rely on paired synthetic datasets, leading to limited generalization performance when applied to real images. In this paper, we propose a novel two-stage unpaired dehazing framework, which unifies real-driven haze transfer and physics-aware constraint to address the domain gap between synthetic and real hazy images. Specifically, in the first stage, a haze-transfer network is designed to generate depth-aware pseudo hazy images by fusing haze characteristics learned from real hazy images with depth cues extracted from unpaired clear images, narrowing the distribution discrepancy between real and synthetic haze. In the second stage, a physics-inspired three-branch network is developed, including a transmission map network, an atmospheric light network, and a scene radiance network. A joint optimization strategy, leveraging pseudo hazy-clear pairs and reconstruction constraints based on the atmospheric model, is proposed to enhance both dehazing performance and model interpretability. Comprehensive experiments conducted on SOTS-outdoor, HSTS, Fattal, and BeDDE datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms many existing state-of-the-art unpaired dehazing methods. The code is available at:https://github.com/Marinerjl/TU-Dehaze.

item.title: OpenMvL: An Attribute-Inspired Dual-Head Framework for Open-Set Multiview Learning
item.dc:creator: Shide Du
Zihan Fang
Lan Du
Yanchao Tan
Changwei Wang
Shiping Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: Numerous researchers have sought to integrate multiview data to enhance task performance. However, existing multiview methods are primarily designed for closed-set environments with fully known classes. In real-world open-set scenarios, the emergence of unknown classes leads to intra-view heterogeneity and inter-view discriminative conflicts. To address these issues, we propose a novel attribute-inspired dual-head framework for open-set multiview learning. First, we develop a multiview attribute-inspired open-set network architecture: 1) A view-specific deep unfolding layer resolves intra-view conflicts by eliminating redundant features and achieving heterogeneity-reduced, consistency-based topological representations for each view; 2) An evidential fusion manner tackles inter-view conflicts by quantifying and integrating global uncertainty, selectively fusing complementary features to create a more discriminative cross-view representation. Furthermore, we design a dual-head perception-adaptive open-set training loss to further bridge these conflicts: 1) the Softmax Softmax -head loss optimizes representation-related parameters and enhances the quality of representation learning, consolidating inherent open-set attributes within the representations, and 2) the Evidence Evidence -head loss optimizes uncertainty-related parameters and calibrates global uncertainty, assisting to disentangle uncertainty from open-set discriminative information. Comprehensive evaluations on challenging open-set multiview tasks demonstrate the method’s outstanding performance, effectively adapting to both known and unknown samples. Published: 2026-07-17T19:45:38+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.780 (must_read)

Authors: Shide Du; Zihan Fang; Lan Du; Yanchao Tan; Changwei Wang; Shiping Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tnnls.2026.3703641

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Numerous researchers have sought to integrate multiview data to enhance task performance. However, existing multiview methods are primarily designed for closed-set environments with fully known classes. In real-world open-set scenarios, the emergence of unknown classes leads to intra-view heterogeneity and inter-view discriminative conflicts. To address these issues, we propose a novel attribute-inspired dual-head framework for open-set multiview learning. First, we develop a multiview attribute-inspired open-set network architecture: 1) A view-specific deep unfolding layer resolves intra-view conflicts by eliminating redundant features and achieving heterogeneity-reduced, consistency-based topological representations for each view; 2) An evidential fusion manner tackles inter-view conflicts by quantifying and integrating global uncertainty, selectively fusing complementary features to create a more discriminative cross-view representation. Furthermore, we design a dual-head perception-adaptive open-set training loss to further bridge these conflicts: 1) the Softmax Softmax -head loss optimizes representation-related parameters and enhances the quality of representation learning, consolidating inherent open-set attributes within the representations, and 2) the Evidence Evidence -head loss optimizes uncertainty-related parameters and calibrates global uncertainty, assisting to disentangle uncertainty from open-set discriminative information. Comprehensive evaluations on challenging open-set multiview tasks demonstrate the method’s outstanding performance, effectively adapting to both known and unknown samples.

item.title: LDDN: A Lightweight Dual-Domain Directional Network for Remote Sensing Change Detection
item.dc:creator: Wei Wang
Shengli Chen
Xin Wang
Xingwei Zhu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Remote sensing image change detection holds significant importance in fields such as urban dynamic monitoring and ecological assessment. However, restricted by parameter capacity, lightweight models struggle to capture subtle changes in complex scenes. Furthermore, factors such as slight registration errors and local textural noise often trigger high-frequency pseudo-change responses, leading to false detections. To this end, this paper proposes a Lightweight Dual-Domain Directional Network (LDDN) for resource-constrained scenarios, achieving efficient and reliable change detection through the synergistic design of frequency-domain noise suppression and spatial-domain directional modeling. Specifically, to suppress unreliable high-frequency responses induced by factors like registration errors, a Directional Wavelet Attention Module (DWAM) is designed. It utilizes the directional high-frequency sub-bands of the discrete wavelet transform to perform adaptive screening, thereby suppressing unreliable components and mining structural cues associated with real changes. To enhance the capability of lightweight models in capturing weak change targets, a Directional Spatial Fusion Module (DSFM) is proposed, which constructs long-range anisotropic modeling capabilities at a low cost by decoupling horizontal and vertical contextual dependencies. Furthermore, to ensure semantic consistency across multiple scales, a Multi-scale Interaction Module (MIM) is introduced to facilitate cross-scale synergy between frequency-domain filtering and spatial-domain directional modeling. Experimental results demonstrate that with only 1.98 M parameters and 2.58 G FLOPs, LDDN achieves competitive or superior detection performance compared to various state-of-the-art methods across four public benchmark datasets. Particularly on the challenging CLCD dataset, the F1-score and IoU are improved by 0.83% and 1.07%, respectively, compared to the second-best method. The code is available at: https:... Published: 2026-07-17T19:44:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.780 (must_read)

Authors: Wei Wang; Shengli Chen; Xin Wang; Xingwei Zhu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3714406

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Remote sensing image change detection holds significant importance in fields such as urban dynamic monitoring and ecological assessment. However, restricted by parameter capacity, lightweight models struggle to capture subtle changes in complex scenes. Furthermore, factors such as slight registration errors and local textural noise often trigger high-frequency pseudo-change responses, leading to false detections. To this end, this paper proposes a Lightweight Dual-Domain Directional Network (LDDN) for resource-constrained scenarios, achieving efficient and reliable change detection through the synergistic design of frequency-domain noise suppression and spatial-domain directional modeling. Specifically, to suppress unreliable high-frequency responses induced by factors like registration errors, a Directional Wavelet Attention Module (DWAM) is designed. It utilizes the directional high-frequency sub-bands of the discrete wavelet transform to perform adaptive screening, thereby suppressing unreliable components and mining structural cues associated with real changes. To enhance the capability of lightweight models in capturing weak change targets, a Directional Spatial Fusion Module (DSFM) is proposed, which constructs long-range anisotropic modeling capabilities at a low cost by decoupling horizontal and vertical contextual dependencies. Furthermore, to ensure semantic consistency across multiple scales, a Multi-scale Interaction Module (MIM) is introduced to facilitate cross-scale synergy between frequency-domain filtering and spatial-domain directional modeling. Experimental results demonstrate that with only 1.98 M parameters and 2.58 G FLOPs, LDDN achieves competitive or superior detection performance compared to various state-of-the-art methods across four public benchmark datasets. Particularly on the challenging CLCD dataset, the F1-score and IoU are improved by 0.83% and 1.07%, respectively, compared to the second-best method. The code is available at: https:...

item.title: Inhibited Self-Attention: Sharpening Focus in Vision Transformers
item.dc:creator: Peter R. D. van der Wal
Nicola Strisciuglio
George Azzopardi
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in computer vision tasks. However, their self-attention mechanism often diffuses focus across background regions, relying on spurious correlations rather than object-relevant cues. Inspired by inhibitory mechanisms observed in biological vision systems, we propose the Inhibited Self-Attention (ISA), a novel self-attention that integrates inhibitory signals to enhance feature selectivity and suppress spurious responses. In contrast to conventional self-attention, which relies solely on positive attention values due to softmax normalization, our approach retains and utilizes negative attention scores to suppress irrelevant features and sharpen focus on objects of interest. Experiments across multiple datasets, including ImageNet-1k and COCO, and several robustness benchmarks demonstrate that ISA enhances object-centric selectivity, reduces shortcut reliance, and improves out-of-distribution generalization. Our analysis of relevance maps confirms that ViTs with ISA exhibit sharper, more localized focus on object-relevant regions while reducing distractions from non-relevant (background) features, enabling more reliable models. We release our code at https://github.com/prdvanderwal/inhibited-self-attention Published: 2026-07-14T15:29:07+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.780 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Peter R. D. van der Wal; Nicola Strisciuglio; George Azzopardi

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in computer vision tasks. However, their self-attention mechanism often diffuses focus across background regions, relying on spurious correlations rather than object-relevant cues. Inspired by inhibitory mechanisms observed in biological vision systems, we propose the Inhibited Self-Attention (ISA), a novel self-attention that integrates inhibitory signals to enhance feature selectivity and suppress spurious responses. In contrast to conventional self-attention, which relies solely on positive attention values due to softmax normalization, our approach retains and utilizes negative attention scores to suppress irrelevant features and sharpen focus on objects of interest. Experiments across multiple datasets, including ImageNet-1k and COCO, and several robustness benchmarks demonstrate that ISA enhances object-centric selectivity, reduces shortcut reliance, and improves out-of-distribution generalization. Our analysis of relevance maps confirms that ViTs with ISA exhibit sharper, more localized focus on object-relevant regions while reducing distractions from non-relevant (background) features, enabling more reliable models. We release our code at https://github.com/prdvanderwal/inhibited-self-attention

item.title: XCT-SAM: Sequential Parameter-Efficient Domain Adaptation of SAM for Industrial XCT Defect Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Md Mahedi Hasan
Md Mushfiqur Rahaman
Alan Pachkovskiy
Imtiaz Ahmed
Jeremy Dawson
Srinjoy Das
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Defect segmentation in additive manufacturing (AM) X-ray computed tomography (XCT) images remains challenging due to severe class imbalance and large distribution shifts across scan conditions. Although recent foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) provide strong general-purpose segmentation priors, their natural-image pre-training transfers poorly to the AM XCT domain, where defects appear as subtle non-semantic microstructural anomalies. Moreover, adapting SAM to the AM domain is further limited by the large domain gap and scarcity of labeled real XCT data. We present XCT-SAM, a sequential parameter-efficient adaptation framework for AM XCT defect segmentation. Instead of adapting SAM directly from natural images to XCT data, we first fine-tune Conv-LoRA adapters on an alloy-microstructure dataset and subsequently transfer the adapted model to XCT images, progressively bridging the domain gap. Using Conv-LoRA adapters with rank r=2, the framework injects convolutional spatial inductive bias into SAM's backbone while training approximately 4.15M parameters and keeping over 99% of the model frozen. We evaluate XCT-SAM on out-of-distribution CycleGAN-XCT benchmarks and real-world NIST XCT scans. Across both settings, XCT-SAM consistently outperforms zero-shot SAM and other domain-adapted SAM baselines, achieving the best overall IoU and Dice scores. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of intermediate domain adaptation with parameter-efficient adapters for industrial XCT defect segmentation. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Mahedi-61/XCT-SAM.git Published: 2026-07-15T18:49:09+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Md Mahedi Hasan; Md Mushfiqur Rahaman; Alan Pachkovskiy; Imtiaz Ahmed; Jeremy Dawson; Srinjoy Das

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Defect segmentation in additive manufacturing (AM) X-ray computed tomography (XCT) images remains challenging due to severe class imbalance and large distribution shifts across scan conditions. Although recent foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) provide strong general-purpose segmentation priors, their natural-image pre-training transfers poorly to the AM XCT domain, where defects appear as subtle non-semantic microstructural anomalies. Moreover, adapting SAM to the AM domain is further limited by the large domain gap and scarcity of labeled real XCT data. We present XCT-SAM, a sequential parameter-efficient adaptation framework for AM XCT defect segmentation. Instead of adapting SAM directly from natural images to XCT data, we first fine-tune Conv-LoRA adapters on an alloy-microstructure dataset and subsequently transfer the adapted model to XCT images, progressively bridging the domain gap. Using Conv-LoRA adapters with rank r=2, the framework injects convolutional spatial inductive bias into SAM's backbone while training approximately 4.15M parameters and keeping over 99% of the model frozen. We evaluate XCT-SAM on out-of-distribution CycleGAN-XCT benchmarks and real-world NIST XCT scans. Across both settings, XCT-SAM consistently outperforms zero-shot SAM and other domain-adapted SAM baselines, achieving the best overall IoU and Dice scores. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of intermediate domain adaptation with parameter-efficient adapters for industrial XCT defect segmentation. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Mahedi-61/XCT-SAM.git

item.title: AVadCLIP: Audio-Visual Collaboration for Robust Video Anomaly Detection
item.dc:creator: Peng Wu
Wanshun Su
Guansong Pang
Yujia Sun
Qingsen Yan
Peng Wang
Yanning Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: With the increasing adoption of video anomaly detection in intelligent surveillance domains, conventional visual-only detection approaches often struggle with information insufficiency and high false-positive rates in complex environments. To address these limitations, we present a novel weakly supervised framework that leverages audio-visual collaboration for robust video anomaly detection. Capitalizing on the exceptional cross-modal representation learning capabilities of Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) across visual, audio, and textual domains, our framework introduces two major innovations: an efficient audio-visual fusion that enables adaptive cross-modal integration through lightweight parametric adaptation while maintaining the frozen CLIP backbone, and a novel audio-visual prompt that dynamically enhances text embeddings with key multimodal information based on the semantic correlation between audio-visual features and textual labels, significantly improving CLIP's generalization for the video anomaly detection task. Moreover, to enhance robustness against modality deficiency during inference, we further develop an uncertainty-driven feature distillation module that synthesizes audio-visual representations from visual-only inputs. This module employs uncertainty modeling based on the diversity of audio-visual features to dynamically emphasize challenging features during the distillation process. Our framework demonstrates superior performance across multiple benchmarks, with audio integration significantly boosting anomaly detection accuracy in various scenarios. Notably, with unimodal data enhanced by uncertainty-driven distillation, our approach consistently outperforms current unimodal VAD methods. Published: 2026-07-17T19:45:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Peng Wu; Wanshun Su; Guansong Pang; Yujia Sun; Qingsen Yan; Peng Wang; Yanning Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3714328

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

With the increasing adoption of video anomaly detection in intelligent surveillance domains, conventional visual-only detection approaches often struggle with information insufficiency and high false-positive rates in complex environments. To address these limitations, we present a novel weakly supervised framework that leverages audio-visual collaboration for robust video anomaly detection. Capitalizing on the exceptional cross-modal representation learning capabilities of Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) across visual, audio, and textual domains, our framework introduces two major innovations: an efficient audio-visual fusion that enables adaptive cross-modal integration through lightweight parametric adaptation while maintaining the frozen CLIP backbone, and a novel audio-visual prompt that dynamically enhances text embeddings with key multimodal information based on the semantic correlation between audio-visual features and textual labels, significantly improving CLIP's generalization for the video anomaly detection task. Moreover, to enhance robustness against modality deficiency during inference, we further develop an uncertainty-driven feature distillation module that synthesizes audio-visual representations from visual-only inputs. This module employs uncertainty modeling based on the diversity of audio-visual features to dynamically emphasize challenging features during the distillation process. Our framework demonstrates superior performance across multiple benchmarks, with audio integration significantly boosting anomaly detection accuracy in various scenarios. Notably, with unimodal data enhanced by uncertainty-driven distillation, our approach consistently outperforms current unimodal VAD methods.

item.title: Diminishing Return of Value Expansion Methods
item.dc:creator: Daniel Palenicek
Michael Lutter
Jo˜ao Carvalho
Daniel Dennert
Faran Ahmad
Jan Peters
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Model-based reinforcement learning aims to increase sample efficiency, but the accuracy of dynamics models and the resulting compounding errors are often seen as key limitations. This paper empirically investigates potential sample efficiency gains from improved dynamics models in model-based value expansion methods. Our study reveals two key findings when using oracle dynamics models to eliminate compounding errors. First, longer rollout horizons enhance sample efficiency, but the improvements quickly diminish with each additional expansion step. Second, increased model accuracy only marginally improves sample efficiency compared to learned models with identical horizons. These diminishing returns in sample efficiency are particularly noteworthy when compared to model-free value expansion methods. These model-free algorithms achieve comparable performance without the computational overhead. Our results suggest that, in the class of state-based, dense reward control tasks studied, the limitation of model-based value expansion methods cannot be attributed solely to model accuracy. Although higher accuracy is beneficial, even perfect models do not provide unrivaled sample efficiency, indicating that other factors limit performance. These results challenge the common assumption that model accuracy is the primary constraint in model-based reinforcement learning, and highlight the need for future work to identify the true limiting factors. Published: 2026-07-16T21:49:18+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Daniel Palenicek; Michael Lutter; Jo˜ao Carvalho; Daniel Dennert; Faran Ahmad; Jan Peters

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3713917

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Model-based reinforcement learning aims to increase sample efficiency, but the accuracy of dynamics models and the resulting compounding errors are often seen as key limitations. This paper empirically investigates potential sample efficiency gains from improved dynamics models in model-based value expansion methods. Our study reveals two key findings when using oracle dynamics models to eliminate compounding errors. First, longer rollout horizons enhance sample efficiency, but the improvements quickly diminish with each additional expansion step. Second, increased model accuracy only marginally improves sample efficiency compared to learned models with identical horizons. These diminishing returns in sample efficiency are particularly noteworthy when compared to model-free value expansion methods. These model-free algorithms achieve comparable performance without the computational overhead. Our results suggest that, in the class of state-based, dense reward control tasks studied, the limitation of model-based value expansion methods cannot be attributed solely to model accuracy. Although higher accuracy is beneficial, even perfect models do not provide unrivaled sample efficiency, indicating that other factors limit performance. These results challenge the common assumption that model accuracy is the primary constraint in model-based reinforcement learning, and highlight the need for future work to identify the true limiting factors.
